content.text: Speculative decoding is a technique for accelerating autoregressive LLM inference without changing the model's output distribution. The key component is the
: a small, fast language model that proposes
autoregressive steps. The large target model then scores all

tokens simultaneously in a single parallel forward pass. Tokens that the target model agrees with are accepted; those it disagrees with are rejected and resampled from the target's distribution. The result is a decode step that produces more than one token while preserving the exact distribution of the target model.
In speculative decoding the pipeline uses two models simultaneously. The

(also called the approximation model or proposal model) is a smaller language model — typically 10–100× fewer parameters than the main model — with identical vocabulary and tokenizer. It runs autoregressively to generate a short sequence of
is the full-size model whose output distribution we want to preserve. Because the target model processes all
draft tokens in one forward pass — reading each position's logits in parallel — it completes that pass in roughly the same time as generating a single token on its own.
The draft model is not merely a heuristic shortcut: the acceptance step uses a rejection-sampling criterion that mathematically guarantees that accepted tokens are drawn from
the target model's distribution. This means speculative decoding is a
inference acceleration technique — unlike quantization or pruning, it never degrades output quality.

at batch size 1. Each autoregressive step generates exactly one token but must load every weight of the model from GPU HBM — a multi-GB transfer that takes roughly the same time regardless of whether one sequence or a full batch is being processed. The problem is that a single forward pass processes so little useful work per byte transferred that GPU arithmetic units sit idle most of the time.
The draft model exploits this wasted arithmetic capacity. While the target model is bandwidth-bound at batch 1, running it at batch

costs nearly the same wall-clock time as batch 1 — because weight transfers dominate, not compute. So if the draft model proposes 4 tokens and the target model verifies them all in one pass, and on average 3 of those tokens are accepted, the effective decode speed triples without any change to the target model's outputs.
How It Works: The Rejection Sampling Protocol
be the draft model's distribution over the next token given context
be the target model's distribution. For each draft token
proposed by the draft model, the acceptance probability is:
, the token is accepted. Otherwise it is rejected and replaced by a sample from the

Critically, no further draft tokens are used after the first rejection: the step terminates, having produced at least one verified token. The mathematics guarantee that the marginal distribution of every accepted token is identical to the target model's — a property proven by
in the original speculative decoding paper.
If the acceptance probability for each position is
(treated as independent and uniform for a rough model), the expected number of accepted tokens in one speculative step is:
, it drops to ≈ 2.3. The speedup is roughly proportional to the acceptance rate, so the draft model quality directly determines how much acceleration you get.

attrs.alt: Speculative decoding draft-verify loop: draft model proposes k tokens, target model verifies in one parallel pass, rejection sampling determines which tokens to accept

content.text: Each speculative step begins with the draft model generating
tokens autoregressively from the current context. These are fed into the target model as a single batched input; the target model produces logits for all

positions simultaneously. The rejection sampler compares target and draft probabilities at each position, accepting or resampling one token at a time from left to right. All accepted tokens are appended to the output and become the new context for the next draft iteration.
Requirements for a Valid Draft Model
Not every small model can serve as a draft model. Three constraints must hold:

The draft and target models must produce logits over the same token set, indexed identically. If the tokenizers differ even by a single added or removed token, the acceptance probability computation is undefined. In practice this means using models from the same family with the same tokenizer checkpoint.

The closer the draft model's distribution is to the target's, the higher the acceptance rate and the greater the speedup. A draft model trained on a different corpus or fine-tuned for a different task can have low acceptance on the target workload, yielding little benefit. Models from the same pretraining run (e.g., Llama-3.1 8B drafting for Llama-3.1 70B) generally achieve acceptance rates of 0.75–0.92 on natural text.
Substantially lower latency than the target.
The draft model must be fast enough that running it

times costs less than one target model pass. If both models take similar time, the overhead of draft generation wipes out any gain from the parallel verification. A 10× size difference (e.g., 7B vs 70B) with the same architecture typically gives enough latency ratio for positive speedup.
Outputs are provably identical in distribution to the target model. No accuracy degradation, no prompt sensitivity, no drift over long generations.

For latency-sensitive single-request serving — the memory-bandwidth-bound regime — speculative decoding is one of the few techniques that genuinely helps, unlike batching which requires concurrent requests.
The target model runs unmodified. The draft model is an external component that can be swapped, retrained, or updated independently.

The draft and target models can each be quantized independently. Speculative decoding can also be combined with continuous batching (though at larger batch sizes the benefit diminishes — see trade-offs).

The draft model must fit in GPU VRAM alongside the target model. A 7B draft model at bf16 requires ~14 GB, which may not fit alongside a 70B target on a single A100 (80 GB). Typical production deployments use 4-bit quantized draft models (~4 GB) to reclaim headroom.
Benefit vanishes at large batch sizes.

At batch 32+, the target model is already compute-bound — tensor cores are saturated and memory bandwidth is no longer the bottleneck. In that regime speculative decoding adds overhead without improving throughput. It is most valuable at batch 1 to batch ~8.
A draft model that achieves 0.85 acceptance on natural prose may fall to 0.55 on code or structured outputs. The speedup varies substantially by task, and some workloads see little benefit.

Operating two models with synchronized KV caches, coordinating draft steps with verification passes, and handling early termination on rejection requires more complex infrastructure than standard decoding.
Several architectures serve as draft models, each with different trade-offs between acceptance rate, memory cost, and implementation complexity:

The original approach from Leviathan et al. and Chen et al. (both 2023): use a separately trained smaller model from the same model family. Examples: Llama-3.1 8B drafts for Llama-3.1 70B; Gemma 2B drafts for Gemma 7B. No architectural changes to either model. Acceptance rate typically 0.75–0.90 on natural text because both models trained on the same data distribution with the same tokenizer.

Medusa (Cai et al., 2024) attaches additional prediction heads directly onto the target model's final hidden states. Each head predicts one additional future token in parallel during the target model's forward pass — no separate model run needed. The trade-off: heads require fine-tuning and the acceptance rate depends on how well the heads can predict multiple tokens ahead from a single hidden state. Memory overhead is small (a few hundred MB for the heads) but the target model must be modified.

EAGLE (Li et al., 2024) trains a single transformer layer on top of the target model's hidden states rather than raw tokens. By conditioning on the target's representations, EAGLE achieves higher acceptance rates than an independent small model at a fraction of the compute cost. It requires access to the target model's internal activations during draft generation, which complicates deployment but enables 3–4× average speedup on benchmarks.

Self-speculative decoding (Zhang et al., 2024) uses the target model itself as the draft model by skipping layers during the draft pass. No separate model is needed; draft tokens are generated with a partial forward pass (e.g., every other layer), reducing draft cost by ~50%. The acceptance rate is typically lower than an independent small model because the draft distribution is a coarser approximation.
How to Implement: Loading Draft and Target Models
The simplest entry point is the Hugging Face Transformers
. Pass a small draft model and the library handles the speculation loop internally:

from transformers import AutoTokenizer, AutoModelForCausalLM
import torch

# Both models must share the same tokenizer
TOKENIZER_ID = "meta-llama/Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct"
DRAFT_MODEL_ID = "meta-llama/Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct"   # draft = 8B
TARGET_MODEL_ID = "meta-llama/Llama-3.1-70B-Instruct"  # target = 70B

tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(TOKENIZER_ID)

# Load draft model on the same device as target (or a separate GPU)
draft_model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained(
    DRAFT_MODEL_ID,
    torch_dtype=torch.bfloat16,
    device_map="cuda:0",
)

# Load target model (may need multiple GPUs or a quantized version)
target_model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained(
    TARGET_MODEL_ID,
    torch_dtype=torch.bfloat16,
    device_map="auto",  # shard across available GPUs
)

prompt = "Explain the difference between TCP and UDP in detail."
inputs = tokenizer(prompt, return_tensors="pt").to("cuda:0")

# Speculative decoding: draft_model proposes, target_model verifies
with torch.no_grad():
    output_ids = target_model.generate(
        **inputs,
        assistant_model=draft_model,   # triggers speculative decoding
        max_new_tokens=256,
        do_sample=False,               # greedy for reproducibility
        num_assistant_tokens=4,        # k: draft tokens per step
    )

print(tokenizer.decode(output_ids[0], skip_special_tokens=True))
— the number of draft tokens generated before each verification pass. Higher values increase throughput when acceptance is high, but each rejected step wastes more draft computation.

Before committing to a draft model in production, measure its empirical acceptance rate on your specific workload. The acceptance rate is not a universal constant — it varies significantly between task types (natural conversation, code, structured output).

import time
import torch
from transformers import AutoTokenizer, AutoModelForCausalLM
from typing import NamedTuple

class SpecDecodeStats(NamedTuple):
    total_tokens: int
    draft_steps: int
    total_accepted: int
    elapsed_seconds: float

    @property
    def acceptance_rate(self) -> float:
        # Average accepted tokens per draft step
        return self.total_accepted / max(self.draft_steps, 1)

    @property
    def tokens_per_second(self) -> float:
        return self.total_tokens / self.elapsed_seconds


def benchmark_speculative(
    target_model,
    draft_model,
    tokenizer,
    prompts: list[str],
    k: int = 4,
    max_new_tokens: int = 200,
) -> SpecDecodeStats:
    total_tokens = 0
    draft_steps = 0
    total_accepted = 0

    t_start = time.perf_counter()
    for prompt in prompts:
        inputs = tokenizer(prompt, return_tensors="pt").to(target_model.device)
        prompt_len = inputs["input_ids"].shape[-1]

        with torch.no_grad():
            out = target_model.generate(
                **inputs,
                assistant_model=draft_model,
                max_new_tokens=max_new_tokens,
                do_sample=False,
                num_assistant_tokens=k,
                return_dict_in_generate=True,
            )

        generated = out.sequences.shape[-1] - prompt_len
        total_tokens += generated
        # Approximation: each generate() call takes ceil(generated / avg_accepted) steps
        # For accurate tracking, instrument the model's _speculative_sampling method

    elapsed = time.perf_counter() - t_start
    return SpecDecodeStats(
        total_tokens=total_tokens,
        draft_steps=draft_steps,       # requires instrumentation for exact count
        total_accepted=total_accepted,
        elapsed_seconds=elapsed,
    )


# Compare standard vs speculative decode
def compare(target_model, draft_model, tokenizer, prompts, k=4, max_new=200):
    # Standard decode
    t0 = time.perf_counter()
    toks_standard = 0
    for p in prompts:
        inp = tokenizer(p, return_tensors="pt").to(target_model.device)
        with torch.no_grad():
            out = target_model.generate(**inp, max_new_tokens=max_new, do_sample=False)
        toks_standard += out.shape[-1] - inp["input_ids"].shape[-1]
    t_standard = time.perf_counter() - t0

    # Speculative decode
    t1 = time.perf_counter()
    toks_spec = 0
    for p in prompts:
        inp = tokenizer(p, return_tensors="pt").to(target_model.device)
        with torch.no_grad():
            out = target_model.generate(
                **inp, assistant_model=draft_model,
                max_new_tokens=max_new, do_sample=False, num_assistant_tokens=k,
            )
        toks_spec += out.shape[-1] - inp["input_ids"].shape[-1]
    t_spec = time.perf_counter() - t1

    print(f"Standard:     {toks_standard/t_standard:.1f} tok/s")
    print(f"Speculative:  {toks_spec/t_spec:.1f} tok/s")
    print(f"Speedup:      {(toks_spec/t_spec) / (toks_standard/t_standard):.2f}x")
Production Use: Speculative Decoding in vLLM
vLLM supports speculative decoding via the
flag. It handles draft-verify scheduling, KV cache management for both models, and integrates cleanly with continuous batching and PagedAttention:

# Launch vLLM server with speculative decoding
# Draft: Llama-3.1-8B, Target: Llama-3.1-70B, k=5
vllm serve meta-llama/Llama-3.1-70B-Instruct \
  --speculative-model meta-llama/Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct \
  --num-speculative-tokens 5 \
  --tensor-parallel-size 4 \
  --dtype bfloat16

# Python API with vLLM offline inference
from vllm import LLM, SamplingParams

llm = LLM(
    model="meta-llama/Llama-3.1-70B-Instruct",
    speculative_model="meta-llama/Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct",
    num_speculative_tokens=5,
    tensor_parallel_size=4,
    dtype="bfloat16",
)

params = SamplingParams(
    temperature=0.0,      # greedy — highest acceptance rate
    max_tokens=512,
)

prompts = [
    "Describe the architecture of a transformer model.",
    "Write a Python function to compute Fibonacci numbers.",
    "What are the key differences between REST and GraphQL?",
]

outputs = llm.generate(prompts, params)
for out in outputs:
    print(out.outputs[0].text)
    # vLLM logs num_accepted_tokens and num_draft_tokens per request
    # in its metrics — check Prometheus endpoint for acceptance rate

Prometheus counters. Track their ratio to monitor acceptance rate in production. If the rate falls below 0.6, consider switching workloads away from speculative decoding or retraining the draft model.
when serving at batch size 1–8, the workload is conversational or document-generation, a same-family smaller model exists, and latency per token (TPOT) is the primary concern.

when GPU memory cannot accommodate both models, you're already running at batch 32+, the workload is highly structured output (JSON/SQL) where the draft model has low acceptance, or you need the simplest possible deployment.
Using a draft model with even a slightly different vocabulary size or token ordering silently produces wrong acceptance probabilities. Always verify:

from transformers import AutoTokenizer

target_tok = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained("meta-llama/Llama-3.1-70B-Instruct")
draft_tok  = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained("meta-llama/Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct")

assert target_tok.vocab_size == draft_tok.vocab_size, (
    f"Vocab mismatch: target={target_tok.vocab_size}, draft={draft_tok.vocab_size}"
)
# Also verify identical special tokens
assert target_tok.eos_token_id == draft_tok.eos_token_id
assert target_tok.bos_token_id == draft_tok.bos_token_id
Using k that is too large

seems appealing — generate more tokens per verification step — but at acceptance rate 0.8, the expected accepted tokens at k=16 is only ~4.5 vs ~3.5 at k=4, while draft compute increases 4×. The formula for optimal
balances draft latency against expected acceptance gain. In practice, k=4 to k=8 is optimal for most settings.
Enabling speculative decoding at high batch size
At batch 64, the target model is already compute-bound. Adding draft generation overhead without bandwidth-bound benefit will
throughput. Measure at your actual batch size before deploying.
Quantize the draft model, not the target.
A 4-bit quantized draft model reduces acceptance rate by only 1–3% while saving ~10 GB VRAM. The target model's full precision is what determines output quality.
Use greedy decoding (temperature=0) for highest acceptance.
At temperature=0, the draft and target models are deterministic and the acceptance probabilities are 0 or 1 — no resampling overhead. High temperatures reduce acceptance rate significantly.
Track the Prometheus metric in production. A drop below 0.65 indicates the workload has drifted from the draft model's distribution — common after prompt engineering changes.
Tune k per workload, not per model.
Code generation often benefits from k=6–8 (code is locally predictable), while creative writing benefits from k=3–4 (higher entropy).

The draft model is the engine of speculative decoding: it generates candidate tokens cheaply so the large target model can verify many at once, exploiting the arithmetic capacity left unused during bandwidth-bound single-token decode. A valid draft model must share the target's tokenizer, have a closely aligned output distribution, and be substantially faster. In practice, same-family smaller models (e.g., 8B drafting for 70B) achieve 1.5–3× decode speedup at batch sizes below 8 with zero loss in output quality. vLLM and Hugging Face Transformers both support speculative decoding with a single configuration flag. The main operational concern is monitoring acceptance rate, which depends on workload type and can vary between 0.6 and 0.92 across tasks.
